retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation -- Imaging:
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography has limited utility. A-mode may help in the screening of maxillary sinus fluid. B-mode may also detect mucosal thickening or soft tissue mass. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Barosinusitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If recurrent symptoms or suspected complications of barosinusitis occur, then non-contrast computed tomography (CT) scanning of the sinuses is indicated for further evaluation and treatment planning. Nasal endoscopy also is diagnostic. In one study, the radiological signs of abnormality were present in over three-quarters of the cases examined. In most of these cases, the maxillary sinus was affected. Of the other cases, the frontal represented one quarter and the ethmoidal in less than a fifth. Of note, although symptoms were predominantly frontal, x-ray changes were most often present in the maxillary sinuses. An x-ray may occasionally show findings such as fluid levels, but the gold standard test is CT. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation -- Imaging:
retrieved_docs.content: Plain film radiographs, including Waters, Caldwell, and Lateral views, may show diffuse opacification, mucosal thickening, or air-fluid levels. However, they are generally unnecessary and unreliable. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation -- Sinus endoscopy:
retrieved_docs.content: Most otolaryngologists prefer rigid sinus endoscopy with video imaging and recording or image capture. Sinus endoscopy helps identify purulence emanating from the middle meatus for culture, nasal polyps, structural abnormalities, fungal disease, or granulomatous disease. This technique is helpful in rare cases for paranasal biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Sinusitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: 1) Diagnosing allergic fungal sinusitis is dependant on major criteria presence of type 1 hypersensitivity, nasal polyps, eosinophilic mucin presence, positive fungal stain, and positive CT findings. Minor criteria of unilateral disease, asthma, and Charcot-Leyden crystals in mucin can further confirm the diagnosis. These patients are young (20 to 30) and have dark-colored rubbery nasal casts. The most common sinus involved is ethmoids. On CT, they show a "double density" sign: thick fungal mucin surrounded by hyperplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, radiographic imaging has limited utility in acute rhinosinusitis unless there is evidence of a complication or an alternative diagnosis. [1] Plain film radiographs are less reliable than coronal computed tomographic (CT) scans when surgical intervention is needed. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is generally not helpful unless a fungal infection or tumor is suspected. Ultrasound offers limited usefulness. Most otolaryngologists prefer fiberoptic sinus endoscopy combined with targeted culture in challenging cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Sinusitis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No laboratory tests are indicated in the emergency department for acute uncomplicated sinusitis because the diagnosis is usually clinical. A plain sinus X-ray may detect the maxillary, frontal, or sphenoid disease but is not useful for evaluating the anterior ethmoid cells or the ostiomeatal complex from which most sinus disease originates. Positive findings on plain films are air-fluid levels, sinus opacity, or mucosal thickening of 6 mm or more. Coronal CT at a thickness of 3 mm to 4 mm is the modality of choice. The CT findings suggest sinusitis is sinus opacification, air-fluid levels, sinus wall displacement, and 4 mm or greater mucosal thickening. [5] Culture and biopsy are indicated for chronic bacterial and fungal sinusitis. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Sinusitis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: 1) In allergic fungal sinusitis (AFS), the microscopic findings of tissue obtained from surgical debridement reveal Charcot-Layden crystals, much like those seen in patients with asthma. Besides, patients have notable polyposis, “eosinophilic appearing mucin” inside the sinuses, and elevated serum IgE levels. [7] Histologically, when observing samples from AFS patients, inflammatory cells are seen inside mucin. Furthermore, samples from AFS patients show “tide lines” or “tree rings” in addition to the characteristics mentioned above. [6] [7] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Squeeze -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging studies are unnecessary unless a complication is suspected, such as a ruptured sinus or pneumocephalus, or if the condition is recurrent. The preferred imaging study is a sinus CT with contrast, although x-ray imaging may also be used. Evaluation by an otolaryngologist with nasal endoscopy can also be diagnostic. Evaluation and imaging studies can reveal causative abnormal anatomies, such as polyps, air-fluid levels, septal deviations, or fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Squeeze -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Barosinusitis can be classified in several ways, as follows. The Weissman classification includes 3 grades of sinus barotraumas, which are classified based on symptoms. Grade I involves mild, temporary sinus discomfort without changes visible on x-rays. Grade II involves severe pain for up to 24 hours, with some mucosal thickening seen on x-ray. Grade III involves severe pain lasting more than 24 hours, along with x-rays showing mucosal thickening or opacification of the sinus; epistaxis or subsequent sinusitis may also be present. A newer classification system has been proposed, which is as follows: (a) acute, isolated barosinusitis; (b) recurrent acute barosinusitis; and (c) chronic barosinusitis. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation -- Imaging:
retrieved_docs.content: MRI scans have limited utility unless there is suspicion of a tumor or fungal infection. MRI is not as specific as CT for detecting bone pathology, but it may be used to evaluate intracranial or ophthalmologic extension of disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoiditis -- Evaluation -- Radiology Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral neck X-ray Computed tomography (CT) of the sinuses

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation -- Imaging:
retrieved_docs.content: A CT scan is considered the gold standard for diagnosing chronic sinusitis. Still, it has poor specificity for acute sinusitis because nearly 50% asymptomatic patients and almost 90% of those with upper respiratory tract infections may show sinus air-fluid levels on the scan. [52] [53] Coronal views are preferred for nasal and sinus anatomy. Delaying the CT scan until after the acute infection is preferred. A limited sinus CT scan may be preferred over a Waters view due to lower radiation exposure, especially in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Acute Rhinosinusitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiological imaging is not routinely recommended to evaluate patients with presumed uncomplicated RARS. However, a noncontrast computed tomography scan of the sinuses is indicated for patients with chronic rhinosinusitis, suspected anatomical abnormalities, or possible orbital complications (see Image. Axial CT of Sphenoid Sinus). Additionally, CT scans are integral to presurgical planning for procedures like balloon sinuplasty and functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS). [19] Research suggests that imaging is overutilized for patients with sinusitis. According to a 2012 study published by Bhattacharyya et al, 11.4% of patients with uncomplicated RARS received a CT scan within 1 year of diagnosis; the number increased to 39.9% at 4 years. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Acute Rhinosinusitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is some etiologic overlap between RARS and chronic rhinosinusitis. Noninvasive fungal pathogens such as Aspergillus fumigatus may be isolated in these patients (see Image. Fungal Sinusitis and Image. Axial View Fungal Sinusitis). Anatomic obstructive abnormalities, including turbinate hypertrophy, conchae bullosae, stenosed sinus ostia, Haller cells (ethmoid cells within the medial orbital floor impinging the maxillary sinus drainage pathway), nasal polyposis, nasal masses, and septal spurs and deviation, can predispose the nasal cavity and sinuses to infection. [5] [6] These structural abnormalities are often correctable through endoscopic surgical techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Sinusitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The three cardinal symptoms of sinusitis are:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: as “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sigmoid sinus” uses the CT head as its finding method and localizes the abnormality to the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus). This demonstrates how CT head imaging can precisely pinpoint bony and vascular lesions within the skull base. Clinically, the CT head examination is associated with سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland carcinoma), indicating that findings on cranial CT may be relevant in the work‑up or staging of this pulmonary malignancy, possibly due to metastatic spread or paraneoplastic phenomena. Overall, routine chest radiography serves as a foundational reference point from which various advanced CT protocols are derived, all of which are integrated within the broader radiographic framework, linked to CNS imaging structures, and applied to both localized cranial pathology and systemic oncologic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2299
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the head that demonstrates mastoid bone erosion of the sigmoid sinus identifies a pathological change located within the sinus durae matris, the dural venous sinus that houses the sigmoid sinus. Recognizing this imaging finding is important for evaluating mastoid disease and its potential impact on the dural venous drainage system.

A specific diagnostic application, “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sinus durae matris,” uses head CT to locate pathology in the mastoid bone and the dural venous sinus. Head CT also co‑occurs with abdominal CT and is associated with “سرطان الغدة الرئوية,” underscoring its utility in comprehensive imaging assessments that may span lung, head, and abdominal regions. Overall, this structured taxonomy unifies bone density testing, a wide spectrum of X‑ray and CT examinations, genitourinary imaging, CNS imaging, and computer‑assisted surgical interventions within the comprehensive category of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. The hierarchical relationships clarify procedural classifications, support accurate coding and documentation, and aid clinicians in selecting appropriate imaging modalities across multilingual

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

The CT head modality itself has a broader relationship with rentgenografie, reinforcing its place within the overall X‑ray imaging spectrum. Within the central nervous system (CNS) imaging hierarchy, the CT head is a child procedure of the parent category إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (CNS imaging procedures). This parent category also includes other specialized studies, such as مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي (abnormal brain MRI plan), illustrating the range of neuro‑imaging techniques available for evaluating intracranial pathology. CT of the head is employed as a diagnostic method for identifying specific anatomical findings. For example, a CT study described as “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sigmoid sinus” uses the CT head as its finding method and localizes the abnormality to the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

In that context, the finding site is the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus), and the imaging method employed is the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The central nervous system imaging category also encompasses the abnormal brain echo mapping procedure (مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي) as a child entity, illustrating the breadth of neuro‑imaging techniques linked to CT head imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Other upper respiratory disease is categorized under the broader group of nasal disorders not elsewhere classified (اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Within this same nasal‑disorder category, epistaxis (الرُعاف) is identified as a specific condition. This hierarchical organization shows that both the general classification of other upper respiratory diseases and the particular manifestation of nosebleeds are subsumed by the overarching nasal disorder category, facilitating systematic diagnosis, documentation, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 195

Diagnostic imaging, specifically الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray), is clinically associated with the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX, highlighting its role in patient assessment. Together, these entities and relationships form an integrated network of therapeutic, diagnostic, and research procedures essential for accurate documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

Autoradiografie is classified as a subtype of rentgenografie, further specifying a particular radiographic technique. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head also shares a broader relationship with rentgenografie and is designated as the primary source for mapping the combined brain‑chest (and abdomen) CT examinations, highlighting the central role of head CT in these multi‑region imaging protocols. Within the domain of central nervous system imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي), the CT scan head is identified as a parent modality. This hierarchical structure includes specific diagnostic applications such as the computed tomography of the head that reveals mastoid bone erosion of the sigmoid sinus. In that context, the finding site is the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus), and the imaging method employed

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2936
retrieved_docs.content: الرُعاف (epistaxis) is classified as a child entity within اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere), indicating that it is a specific type of nasal disorder that does not fit into other subcategories. Clinical attention to this condition centers on the control of epistaxis, which is identified as a primary focus of management for patients experiencing nasal bleeding. Recognizing الرُعاف as a distinct subset of unclassified nasal disorders facilitates targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at effectively controlling bleeding and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: The finding described as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion” is classified as an “اضطراب منصفي” (segmental disorder). This indicates that the presence of fluid accumulation in the mediastinum identified on chest CT represents a localized, segmental pathological process. Recognizing mediastinal effusion on imaging as a segmental disorder helps clinicians diagnose and manage thoracic conditions that are confined to specific anatomical regions.
